title: Running Gemma 4 Locally: The Complete LM Studio Setup Guide
excerpt: LM Studio's headless CLI now lets you run Google's Gemma 4 as a local API endpoint, then connect Claude Code to it for an entirely on-device coding agent. No cloud costs, no API keys, no data leaving your machine.
author: AIToolCrunch
content:
The appeal is straightforward: a capable AI coding agent that costs nothing per token, stores nothing in the cloud, and runs entirely on hardware you already own. This setup used to require patchwork configurations and custom scripting. Now it's legitimate enough that developers on Hacker News are sharing working examples.

Why local inference matters right now

Cloud API pricing has become a real budget constraint for power users. Claude Code users regularly hit the $200/month ceiling. GitHub Copilot Pro costs $20/month on top of GitHub Pro. If you're running dozens of coding sessions daily, those costs compound.

But this isn't just about cost. Three practical motivations drive developers toward local setups:

  • Zero per-token billing - run unlimited generations after the initial hardware investment
  • Complete data privacy - proprietary code never leaves your machine or hits a company's servers
  • Predictable performance - no rate limits, no queues, no waiting for cloud infrastructure

Until recently, the friction of local setup made these benefits theoretical for most developers. LM Studio's new headless CLI changes that calculation.

What you're actually running: Gemma 4 specifics

Google released Gemma 4 in early 2026 as an open-weight model family - meaning the model weights are publicly available and you can run them locally, unlike Claude or GPT-4o which exist only on company servers.

The family includes four sizes:

Model Parameters Minimum RAM Best For
Gemma 4 1B 1 billion 4GB Mobile, resource-constrained
Gemma 4 4B 4 billion 8GB Laptops, lightweight tasks
Gemma 4 12B 12 billion 16GB Mac M1/M2, everyday coding
Gemma 4 27B 27 billion 32GB+ Complex tasks, M3 Pro and up

On a MacBook Pro M4 Max, the 27B variant runs at 30-40 tokens per second. Genuinely usable. The 12B version on an M3 Air handles autocomplete, code review, and simple refactors without lag. Both outperform what you'd expect from the parameter count, thanks to Google's training optimizations.

The realistic assessment: solid for routine tasks, weaker on architectural reasoning and deep contextual code understanding. If 70% of your coding queries are straightforward - write this function, explain this pattern, find the obvious bug - local Gemma handles that tier comfortably. The remaining 30% might still justify cloud API access.

LM Studio's headless mode: what changed

LM Studio existed as a desktop GUI for running local models. Useful, but limited. You'd start the app, load a model, manually configure other tools to connect to it. The new headless CLI removes the GUI entirely and runs as a clean background server.

The important detail: it exposes an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. Most AI coding tools - Cursor, Goose, Claude Code, and others - can connect to any OpenAI-compatible backend. Once your local model runs as an API, these tools use it transparently, identical to how they'd use the real OpenAI or Anthropic API.

This is the architectural change that makes local setup practical. You're not retrofitting broken integrations. You're using the same interface, just pointing it at localhost instead of api.openai.com.

The actual setup process

First: install and download the model. Get LM Studio from lmstudio.ai, open it, search for Gemma 4, and download the variant matching your hardware. The 12B model is around 7GB compressed. Budget accordingly for your connection.

Second: start the server with headless mode.

lms server start --model gemma-4-12b

This launches the model as an OpenAI-compatible API at http://localhost:1234/v1 by default. The server runs indefinitely in the background. You'll see token generation speed logged to your terminal - useful for verifying performance.

Third: configure Claude Code or your chosen tool. Most tools accept these environment variables:

export ANTHROPIC_API_BASE_URL=http://localhost:1234/v1
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=local

LM Studio doesn't use API key authentication locally, so any value works for the key. Launch Claude Code. The interface looks identical - you're simply using a different model backend underneath.

Total friction: maybe 10 minutes if you're familiar with the command line, 20-30 if not.

Real performance expectations

This varies dramatically by hardware. M4 Max with 48GB? The 27B model feels nearly responsive enough to forget it's local. M3 Air with 16GB? The 12B model works, but you notice the latency difference compared to cloud API calls.

More important than raw speed: what types of problems can you actually solve?

Gemma 4 handles these confidently:

  • Generating functions from docstrings or comments
  • Explaining existing code passages
  • Suggesting refactors for obvious issues
  • Writing basic tests
  • Catching syntax errors

Gemma 4 struggles with:

  • Deep architectural reasoning across large codebases
  • Unfamiliar or obscure frameworks
  • Tasks requiring extensive context retention
  • Complex multi-step refactors

The hybrid strategy works best: use local Gemma for routine queries, reserve cloud API access for problems that require stronger reasoning. You'll reduce cloud costs by 70-80% while keeping quality where it matters.

Privacy as the secondary benefit

Everything stays on your machine. Prompts don't hit company servers. Your proprietary code doesn't pass through cloud infrastructure. For developers bound by data residency requirements or working on genuinely sensitive systems, this moves from nice-to-have to essential.

This is partly why Goose gained traction with enterprise teams - it's built from the ground up to support local backends, meaning you get autonomous coding agents without any cloud exposure.

Beyond Gemma: other models worth considering

Gemma isn't your only option. LM Studio runs these equally well:

Mistral Small 3 - Excellent multilingual support, strong coding performance, 22B parameters. Comfortable on 32GB machines.

DeepSeek Coder V3 - Purpose-built for coding, competitive with Gemma on most benchmarks, slightly smaller footprint.

Phi-4 - Microsoft's 14B model that performs above its weight on reasoning tasks, remarkably fast on consumer hardware.

LM Studio handles all of them with identical setup. The best model depends entirely on testing against your actual codebase and hardware. Benchmarks matter less than how the model performs on code you care about.

When to go local vs. cloud

Go local if you're hitting cloud API cost ceilings, working with proprietary code under regulatory constraints, or want completely predictable latency. The tradeoff is model capability ceiling - local models don't yet match Claude Opus or GPT-4o on genuinely hard problems.

Stay cloud if you're doing occasional coding, need state-of-the-art reasoning, or don't have hardware that meets the 16GB minimum. A single month of Claude Code usage might cost less than the laptop upgrade required to run 27B models comfortably.

The practical answer for most developers: both. Route routine tasks through local Gemma, keep cloud API access for the hard problems. LM Studio makes that hybrid approach straightforward now.
